zh.lifeSummary: 當William Oliver於1510年出生在英國萊希斯特斯爾可希頓時，他的父親William Oliver20歲，母親Dorothy Oliver11歲。他於1543年與Isabel Majors在英國英格蘭萊希斯特斯爾結婚。他們至少是1個女兒的父母。他去世於1603年11月1日在英國英格蘭米德萊可特查阿培爾享年93歲。

nameMeanings.name: Oliver
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (northern), southern Scottish, southern French, and German: from the Old French personal name Oliver (modern French Olivier). This became common largely through the influence of the immensely popular narrative poem Chanson de Roland, in which the warrior Oliver is the wise best friend of the doomed hero Roland. The two men were peers at the court of Charlemagne, and the name is probably of ancient Germanic origin. The name ostensibly means ‘olive tree’ (see Oliveira ), but this is almost certainly the result of folk etymology working on an unidentified ancient Germanic personal name, perhaps a cognate of Alvaro . Old Norse Óláf (see Olliff ) and ancient Germanic Alfhari (composed of the elements alf ‘elf’ and hari ‘army’) have also been suggested as sources, but both personal names are difficult to explain phonetically, especially the latter. The Anglo-Norman name has been established in Ireland (Louth) since at least the 14th century, and was reinforced in Ulster and Limerick by migrants from England in the 17th century. The surname is also borne by Jews, apparently as an adoption of the non-Jewish surname.
Catalan: generally a topographic name from oliver ‘olive tree’, but in some instances possibly related to a homonymous personal name (see 1 above).
History: The surname Oliver of French origin (see 1 above) is listed in the register of Huguenot ancestors recognized by the Huguenot Society of South Carolina.

variants.name: Oliveros
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Francisco, Pedro, Alfredo, Gilberto, Juan, Arturo, Carlos, Javier, Jesus, Jorge, Luis.
Spanish: from an old variant of the personal name Olivero (see Oliver ).

variants.name: Oliveira
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Manuel, Jose, Carlos, Luis, Fernando, Mario, Luiz, Francisco, Sergio, Ana, Geraldo, Pedro.  Portuguese Joao, Paulo, Joaquim, Duarte, Agostinho, Guilherme, Marcio, Sebastiao, Anabela, Henrique, Messias, Ademir.

Portuguese and Galician: habitational name from any of numerous places called Oliveira, from Portuguese and Galician oliveira ‘olive grove’ (from Late Latin olivarius, a derivative of Latin oliva ‘olive’), or a topographic name from this word. Compare De Oliveira .

Jewish (Sephardic): adoption of the Iberian surname (see 1 above) at the moment of conversion to Roman Catholicism. After the return to Judaism (generations later), some descendants retained the name their families used as Catholics.

variants.name: Oliviero
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Antonio, Amedeo, Aniello, Ciro, Dante, Gennaro, Pasquale, Pietro, Rocco, Salvatore, Silvio, Ugo.  Spanish Mario, Ademar, Guadalupe, Jose, Ricardo.
Italian: from the personal name Oliviero, from the Old French personal name Olivier (see Oliver ).

variants.name: Olivier
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Pierre, Jacques, Michel, Andre, Antoine, Julien, Marcel, Armand, Benoit, Edouard, Emile, Henri.
French: from the personal name Olivier, standard French form of Oliver .

variants.name: Olver
meanings: English (Cornwall and Devon): variant of   Oliver  .

variants.name: Olivero
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Luis, Jose, Mercedes, Ana, Andres, Armando, Cesar, Domitila, Dulce, Feliciano, Fernanda, Fernando.  Italian Antonio, Aldo, Carlo, Carmela, Carmelo, Franco, Gabriele, Marco.
Spanish and Italian: from the personal name Olivero (see Oliver ).
Italian: from Old Italian olivero, which, like olivaro, was used to denote an olive tree, and may also have been applied as a nickname for someone who owned or cultivated olive trees.

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

historicEvents.title: Book of Common Prayer
historicEvents.description: Book of Common Prayer, a product of the English Reformation, was published in 1549 for assistance in the administration of the sacraments and other rites and ceremonies of the church according to the use of the Church of England. The book outlined morning, evening, and communion prayers and orders for baptism and marriage, thus making England a truly Protestant state.

historicEvents.title: Act of Uniformity
historicEvents.description: The Act of Uniformity was passed by the Parliament of England and required all people to go to church once a week. The consequence of not attending church was a fine of 12 pence, which was a considerable amount for a poor person.

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

historicEvents.title: The Royal Exchange is Organized
historicEvents.description: The Royal Exchange was organized in January 1571 by Sir Thomas Gresham. Gresham is known as the father of English banking.

historicEvents.title: The First English Colony is Established in America
historicEvents.description: England established its first colony in North America in 1585 as a way to generate additional wealth. The colony was named Virginia, after the virgin Queen Elizabeth I.

historicEvents.title: Elizabeth I Signs Death Warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots
historicEvents.description: Elizabeth I signed the death warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots, on February 1, 1587, as she was found guilty of plotting to assasinate Elizabeth. Mary, Queen of Scots, was beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle in England on February 8, 1587.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Elizabeth I.org - Mary Queen of Scots

en.lifeSummary: When William Oliver was born in 1510, in Cossington, Leicestershire, England, his father, William Oliver, was 20 and his mother, Dorothy Oliver, was 11. He married Isabel Majors in 1543, in Leicestershire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 1 daughter. He died on 1 November 1603, in Whitechapel, Middlesex, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 93.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque William Oliver est né en 1510, à Cossington, Leicestershire, Angleterre, son père, William Oliver, avait 20 ans et sa mère, Dorothy Oliver, avait 11 ans. Il a épousé Isabel Majors en 1543, à Leicestershire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fille. Il est décédé le 1 novembre 1603, à Whitechapel, Middlesex, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 93 ans.

November 1603 im Alter von 93 Jahren in Whitechapel, Middlesex, England, United Kingdom.

Morì il 1 novembre 1603, nel Whitechapel, Middlesex, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, all'età di 93 anni.
